coredata.dc:title: A label-free fluorescent sensor based on carbon quantum dots with enhanced sensitive for the determination of myricetin in real samples
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Lizhen
Mi, Zhi
Guo, Ziying
Wang, Junling
Feng, Feng
coredata.dc:description: In this research, a novel label-free fluorescent sensor for the determination of myricetin was designed based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The CQDs were prepared via green and straightforward microwave-assisted heating method using aspartic acid and urea as precursors. The prepared CQDs showed strong fluorescence and good solubility in water. The sensing principle of sensor is the quenching of CQDs fluorescence by myricetin via inner filter effect (IFE). The developed CQDs sensor was sensitive to myricetin with a wide linear detection range of 1 to 80 µM and a low limit of detection (LOD) of 18.4 nM. The potential interfering substances showed no influence on the determination of myricetin. Furthermore, the proposed sensor based on CQDs was successfully applied for the determination of myricetin in red wine samples and exhibited simple operation, low cost and high accuracy. The results indicated our developed sensor based on CQDs has great application prospects for myricetin detection in real sample analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescent sensor
Myricetin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analytical performance of the CQDs for myricetin detection
Quenching mechanism of the CQDs by myricetin
Detection of myricetin in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CQDs and (B) size distribution of CQDs.
XPS spectrum of CQDs. (A) Full scan spectrum, (B) C1s spectrum, (C) N1s spectrum and (D) O1s spectrum.

(A) UV-vis absorption spectra (blue line), excitation spectra (red line) and emission spectra (black line) of the CQDs solution. Inset: photograph of the CQDs solution under daylight lamp (left) and UV-visible light lamp (right). (B) The fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs at different excitation wavelengths (310–500 nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs at different myricetin concentrations (0-80 µM). (B) The linear relationship between F0/F and the concentration of myricetin. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three independent experiments.

The F0/F of CQDs in the presence of myricetin (30 µM), metal ions (100 µM), amino acid (100 µM) and other co-existing substances (100 µM). The error bars represent the standard deviation of three independent experiments.
Analytical results of myricetin in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A label-free fluorescent sensor based on carbon quantum dots with enhanced sensitive for the determination of myricetin in real samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this research, a novel label-free fluorescent sensor for the determination of myricetin was designed based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The CQDs were prepared via green and straightforward microwave-assisted heating method using aspartic acid and urea as precursors. The prepared CQDs showed strong fluorescence and good solubility in water. The sensing principle of sensor is the quenching of CQDs fluorescence by myricetin via inner filter effect (IFE). The developed CQDs sensor was sensitive to myricetin with a wide linear detection range of 1 to 80 µM and a low limit of detection (LOD) of 18.4 nM. The potential interfering substances showed no influence on the determination of myricetin. Furthermore, the proposed sensor based on CQDs was successfully applied for the determination of myricetin in red wine samples and exhibited simple operation, low cost and high accuracy. The results indicated our developed sensor based on CQDs has great application prospects for myricetin detection in real sample analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Myricetin (Fig. S1), a natural flavonoid compound with six hydroxyl substitutions, is widespread in plants such as vegetables, fruits, berries, tea and medicinal herbs. Recent studies revealed beneficial effects of myricetin on human health. Clinical studies have shown that myricetin is a natural α-glucosidase inhibitor and a class B GPCR agonist, with a promising usage as therapeutic agent for type 2 diabetes [1,2]. Moreover, myricetin has been proved to be a bioactive ingredient that can be used to inhibit platelet aggregation [3]. Other reported health-promoting effects of myricetin include anti-cancer and anti-inflammatory activities [4,5]. However, some studies indicate that the excessive intake of myricetin might cause pulmonary fibrosis [6], due to its poor water solubility and bioavailability. In order to make full use of myricetin and weaken its dose-limiting side effect, it is important to establish a simple, sensitive, fast and accurate analytical method for myricetin determination.

At present, the methods for myricetin determination mainly include thin-layer chromatography (TLC) [7], flow injection chemiluminescence (FICL) [8], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) [9], high performance liquid chromatography-ultra violet (HPLC-UV) [10,11], HPLC-diode array detector (HPLC-DAD) [12,13], HPLC-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) [14], electrochemical (EC) [15,16] and capillary electrophoresis (CE) [17] methods. Although these methods can be effectively used for the determination of myricetin, the majority of them have certain drawbacks such as long analysis time, complicated operations, high labor intensity and expensive instruments [18]. In comparison with these methods, the fluorescence sensor method has attracted great attention due to its fast response, ease operation, low cost, free from complicated pretreatment and excellent sensitivity [19].

Recently, Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted significant attention as a novel fluorescent nanomaterial with several splendid properties such as high fluorescence, tunable photoluminescence, excellent chemical stability, low cost, facile preparation and ease of functionalization [20]. Compared with semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes, CQDs are green materials with good water-solubility, light stability, low toxicity, and high biocompatibility [21,22]. These unique properties make CQDs widely used in bio-imaging [23,24], drug delivery [25,26], photo-catalysis [27,28], electrocatalysis [29,30] and sensing [31,32]. In particular, CQDs have shown superior applications in chemistry and biological sensing, which can be used to detect a variety of substances such as adenosine 5´-triphosphate [33], sialic acid [34], amaranth [35], guanine [36], morin [37], perfluorooctane sulfonate [38], Hg2+ [39], Al3+ [40] and Ag+ [41]. However, as far as we know, there are no reports on the use of CQDs-based fluorescence sensor for the detection of myricetin.

In this research, for the first time, we report a CQDs-based fluorescence sensor for the selective and sensitive determination of myricetin. The CQDs were produced by microwave-assisted pyrolysis of aspartic acid and urea. This green synthesis method is simple, economical and fast. The synthesized CQDs have uniform particle size, good dispersion, exceptional solubility in water (20 °C, 6 g/100 mL) and strong fluorescence. It is discovered that myricetin can strongly quench the fluorescence emission of CQDs via inner filter effect (IFE). Based on this quenching effect, a new fluorescent sensor for the determination of myricetin was developed. The sensor showed a wide linear detection range (1–80 µM) and low LOD (18.4 nM). Finally, the developed CQDs-based fluorescence sensor proved to be efficient for the determination of myricetin in red wine and human serum samples.

Myricetin, ferulic acid, cysteine, histidine, threonine, glutamic acid, alanine, tryptophan, glycine, rutin, chrysophanol, chlorogenic, daidzein and aspartic acid were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Limited Company (Shanghai, China). Quinine sulfate, urea and acetonitrile were supplied by Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Limited Company (Shanghai, China). Potassium bromide (KBr), hydrochloric acid (HCl), disodium phosphate (Na2HPO4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4) were acquired from Shanghai Jinsui Biotechnology Limited Company (Shanghai, China). CaCl2, ZnCl2, KCl, AlCl3, PbCl2, NaCl and MgCl2 were purchased from Shanghai Jizhi Biochemical Technology Limited Company (Shanghai, China). All reagents were analytical grade and used as received. For all the experiments, ultra-pure water was used. 3 mM myricetin stock solution was prepared in methanol and kept at 4 °C before use. 0.01 M phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solution was applied to control the acidity.

The TEM image was acquired using a Tecnai GI F20 transmission electron microscope (USA) operated at 200 kV accelerating voltage. The sample for TEM was prepared by adding a drop of CQDs solution to the Agar carbon-coated 400 mesh Cu grid. The IR spectra of CQDs were recorded in 500–4000 cm−1 range on a Nicolet Magna-IR 550 FTIR spectrometer (USA). The KBr pellets were prepared by mixing a few milligrams of CQDs sample with around 100 mg of KBr, powdering in an agate mortar to about 2 µm, and applying a pressure of 29.7 MPa using a press. The XPS was obtained on a Thermo Fisher K-Alpha photoelectron spectrometer (USA). The spectral data were analyzed by Casa XPS v.2.3.12 program, using the peak fitting option with the symmetric Gaussian-Lorentzian function. The UV-vis spectra of CQDs sample were recorded on a Lambda 35 UV-Vis absorption spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA) over 200–700 nm range. The fluorescence spectra of CQDs sample were collected on a F-2500 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan) which use Xe lamp as an excitation light source.

The CQDs were synthesized via straightforward, microwave-assisted heating procedure. Briefly, 1.0 g aspartic acid was stirred with 1.0 g urea in 10 mL ultra-pure H2O. The solution was heated in a microwave oven at 600 W for 4 min. After the heating was finished, the resultant was diluted in 50 mL ultra-pure H2O and purified by a dialysis membrane (500 Da) in ultra-pure H2O for 24 h. The final CQDs products were obtained from the dialyzed CQDs solutions after freeze-drying for 48 h.

The quantum yield (ΦS ) of synthesized CQDs sample was obtained by comparing the absorbance and integrated fluorescence intensities of CQDs and quinine sulfate (reference compound). The solution of quinine sulfate (ΦR = 0.54) was prepared in 0.10 M sulfuric acid (refractive index, η = 1.33), while CQDs solutions with different concentrations were prepared by serial dilution with ultra-pure H2O (η = 1.33). The absorbance was recorded at 350 nm (A350), and the same wavelength was used as an excitation wavelength for fluorescence spectra. The emission intensity was recorded and integrated in the wavelength range 370–620 nm. The ΦS of CQDs was calculated from the graph of integrated fluorescence intensity (IFI) vs. A350 according to the equation:
(1)
Φ
S
=
Φ
R
(
G r a
d S
/ G r a
d R
)
(
η 2
S
/
η 2
R
)
where subscripts S and R denote sample (CQDs) and reference (quinine sulfate). Grad represents the slope of IFI vs. A plot, and η is the refractive index of the solvent. The A350 values of all solutions measured in 10-mm path length fluorescence quartz cuvette were kept under 0.10, providing a negligible self-absorption effect [42,43].
Red wine samples are from Great-wall® red wine which were provided by a local supermarket and used without any sample preparation.

Human blood samples were collected from the Affiliated Hospital of Shanxi Datong University. The blood samples were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to isolate the serum. 1 mL serum samples and 1 mL acetonitrile were mixed in a centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 20 min to remove the proteins. The supernatant was collected and the residue of acetonitrile was removed by N2 gas and kept at 4 °C for further analysis.

The 0.01 M PBS solution (pH = 6) was used as a medium for the detection of myricetin. The usual experimental run comprises the mixing of 1.2 mL CQDs solution (5 mg/mL) with different volumes of myricetin stock solution or sample solution, and the dilution of each mixture to 3 mL with 0.01 M PBS solution (pH = 6). After incubating at room temperature for 15 min, the fluorescence spectrum was obtained by setting the excitation wavelength at 350 nm. The relative fluorescence intensity (F0 /F), i.e. the ratio of fluorescence intensity of CQDs with (F) and without myricetin (F 0) was taken as an analytical signal of myricetin. The selectivity of CQDs to myricetin was performed by recording the spectra of other competitive substances following the same procedure.

The morphology and size of CQDs particles were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 , the CQDs particles are spherical and uniformly distributed in the sample. The size distribution of CQDs ranges from 1.5 to 3.8 nm, with an average diameter of 2.8 nm.

To gain insights into the chemical composition and surface functional groups of synthesized CQDs, the IR and XPS spectra were investigated. The IR spectrum of CQDs is displayed in Fig. S2. The characteristic, broad and intense peak originating from N-H and O-H stretching vibrations is observed between 3000 and 3500 cm−1 [44]. The peaks observed at 1718, 1655 and 1184 cm−1 correspond to C=O, C=C and C-N stretching vibrations, respectively [45]. The signal at 1006 cm−1 is related to C-O stretching, while signal at 1403 cm−1 originates from N-H bending vibration. The XPS spectrum of CQDs is illustrated in Fig. 2 . Three characteristic peaks at 288.68, 400.13 and 532.08 eV are visible on XPS full scan spectrum (Fig. 2A), which originate from C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2B) was decomposed into four signals at 288.78, 287.03, 286.48 and 285.53 eV, corresponding to C=O, C-O, C-N and C-C, respectively [46,47]. The N1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2C) exhibits two signals at 402.13 and 400.18 eV, originating from N-H and C-N, respectively [48]. The O1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2D) displays three signals at 534.13, 532.08 and 531.78 eV, originating from O-H, C=O and C-O, respectively [46,47]. The above IR and XPS results suggest that hydroxyl (-OH), carboxyl (-COOH) and amino (-NH2) functional groups exist on the surface of CQDs.

With the increase of λex from 310 to 500 nm, the emission maxima of CQDs red shifts from 443 to 557 nm. This excitation-dependent emission property of CQDs is a result of the existence of several structurally different fluorophores on the surface of CQDs and different particle sizes of CQDs [39].

The dosages of CQDs, pH of aqueous solution and incubation time are important factors for sensing performance of CQDs toward myricetin. Therefore, these three experimental parameters were tested in order to gain the maximum response of the CQDs to myricetin. The relative fluorescence intensity (F0 /F) was was used to evaluate the response of the CQDs to myricetin in each experiment.

Fig. S4A depicts the response of the CQDs to myricetin at different dosage of CQDs in the range of 0.3-2.4 mL. With the increase of CQDs dosage from 0.3 to 1.2 mL, the response of the CQDs to myricetin increased gradually, while higher dosages diminished the response. The maximum response of the CQDs to myricetin was obtained when the dosage of CQDs was 1.2 mL. Thus, 1.2 mL was considered to be the optimum dosage of CQDs for the determination of myricetin in the following experiments.

Fig. S4B shows the pH-dependent response of the CQDs to myricetin between pH 2 and 12. The response of the CQDs to myricetin increases gradually from pH 2 to 6, reaches the maximum value at pH 6, and then decreases when the pH changes from 6 to 12. Therefore, pH 6 was chosen as the optimum value for the determination of myricetin in the following experiments.

Fig. S4C displays the response of the CQDs to myricetin at different incubation time in the range of 0-60 min. The response of the CQDs to myricetin increases gradually within 15 min, while longer incubation times didn't change the analytical signal. Therefore, 15 min was selected as the optimum incubation time for the determination of myricetin in the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the CQDs for myricetin detection
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimum conditions, the fluorescence signal of CQDs was recorded at various myricetin concentrations. As myricetin concentration increases from 0 to 80 µM, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs decreases gradually (Fig. 4 A). The strong linear dependence between F0 /F and concentration of myricetin in 1-80 µM range is illustrated in Fig. 4B. The following regression equation is obtained: F0 /F = 0.0381[C] + 1, where [C] is the concentration of myricetin (µM). The correlation coefficient (R2) is 0.9927, indicating good linear relationship between F0 /F and [C]. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as a value where signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) is 3. The calculated LOD for myricetin was 18.4 nm.

The overview of other reported methods for myricetin detection and comparison with our developed method is given in Table S1 [9,11,12,16,17]. It can be seen that our method exhibit lower LOD than other reported methods. It should be mentioned that our method based on CQDs is simple, inexpensive, rapid response and environmental-friendly. Thus, it has a good application prospect in the detection of myricetin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanism of the CQDs by myricetin
ce:section.ce:para:
Some studies were performed to understand the quenching mechanism of the CQDs by myricetin. As shown in Fig. S5A, the UV-Vis spectrum of myricetin exhibits a characteristic absorption peak at 376 nm, which lies in the wavelength range where CQDs show excitation and emission peaks. This indicates that the quenching mechanism may be caused by IFE or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [48]. IFE does not involve changes in fluorescence lifetime, while FRET is accompanied by changes in fluorescence lifetime [53]. Fig. S5B shows the fluorescence decay curves of CQDs with and without myricetin. In order to ensure the accuracy of the lifetime measurements, three independent measurements were performed for each sample. A bi-exponential fit was used to estimate the lifetimes of CQDs using the following equation:
(2)
τ =
A 1
τ 1
2
+
A 2
τ 2
2
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2
where τ is the fluorescence lifetime, τ1 and τ2 are the time constants of the two radiative decay channels, and A1 and A2 are the corresponding amplitudes, respectively. The corresponding data are summarized in Table S2. The average fluorescence lifetimes of CQDs before and after the addition of myricetin are determined to be 4.77 and 4.75 ns, respectively. The fluorescence lifetimes of CQDs in the absence and presence of myricetin are essentially unchanged, which clearly verified that there is no energy transfer process between CQDs and myricetin [54]. In other words, it can exclude the occurrence of FRET between CQDs and myricetin. The result indicates that IFE-based quenching mechanism occurred.

To verify IFE-based quenching mechanism, corrections are made by considering the cuvette geometry and absorption features of the mixtures of CQDs and myricetin using the following equation [55-57]:
(3)
F
c o r
F
o b s d
=
2.3 d
A
e x
1 −
10
− d
A
e x
10
g
A
e m
2.3 s
A
e m
1 −
10
− s
A
e m
Where Fobsd is the observed fluorescence intensity, Fcor represents the corrected fluorescence intensity after removing IFE from Fobsd. Aex and Aem stand for absorbance at the maximum excitation wavelength (350 nm) and maximum emission wavelength (450 nm), respectively. g is the distance between the edge of the excitation beam and edge of cuvette (0.40 cm), s is the thickness of the excitation beam (0.10 cm), and d is the width of the cuvette (1.00 cm). Parameters used to calculate the IFE of myricetin on the fluorescence of CQDs are summarized in Table S3. Fcor was obtained according to eq 3. The suppressed efficiency for the observed and corrected fluorescence of CQDs was calculated by removing the IFE. It is found that about 90 % of suppressed efficiency comes from IFE. To additionally confirm the IFE as the main quenching mechanism of CQDs fluorescence by myricetin, the absorbances of the reaction system with different concentrations of myricetin are recorded at 376 nm (Fig. S5C). The gradual increase in A376 value with the increase in myricetin concentration and a good linear relationship between these two parameters further suggests that the main quenching mechanism is IFE [58].

To test the specificity of the CQDs toward myricetin, the influences of several metal ions (Zn2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Na+, K+ and Al3+), amino acid (cysteine, histidine, threonine, glutamic acid, alanine, tryptophan and glycine) and other co-existing substances (chlorogenic, chrysophanol, rutin, daidzein and ferulic acid) were examined. As can be seen from Fig. 5 , the influence of interfering substances on the analytical signal of CQDs is negligible, indicating high selectivity of CQDs-based fluorescence sensor for myricetin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of myricetin in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical applicability of CQDs-based fluorescence sensor was tested for the detection of myricetin in the real red wine and human serum samples. The analytical data are listed in Table 1 . The accuracy and precision of the method were confirmed by the values of recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD), which ranged from 97.5 to 105 % and 1.4 to 2.9 %, respectively. The results indicated that our fabricated sensor has good accuracy, reliability and practicability, and can be successfully applied to the determination of myricetin in real samples.

In summary, a novel label-free fluorescent sensor for the detection of myricetin based on CQDs was successfully constructed. The CQDs were produced via straightforward microwave-assisted heating method using aspartic acid and urea as precursors. The obtained CQDs have good solubility in water and strong fluorescence light emission. Such prepared CQDs proved to be a sensitive and selective fluorescence sensor for the determination of myricetin. It successfully demonstrated that the fluorescence sensor for the determination of myricetin is based on mechanism of IFE. Finally, the proposed sensor based on CQDs was applied to determination of myricetin in red wine and human serum samples with satisfactory results obtained. The developed sensor has advantages of simple operation, low cost, rapid, label-free, easy to use, high sensitivity and good selectivity, and shows great potential in the detection of myricetin.
All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial supports from Applied Basic Research Project Fund of Shanxi Province (201901D211434), Higher Education Technology Innovation Project of Shanxi Province (2019L0750), Industry Key Research and Development Plan Project Fund of Datong City (2018014) and the Doctoral Science Research Fund of Shanxi Datong University (2016-B-13).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.microc.2020.104956.
Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j. microc.
